author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
Even Pious and God-Fearing Men Resist Grace.
title: Even Pious and God-Fearing Men Resist Grace.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Chapter 58 [L.]—Even Pious and God-Fearing Men Resist Grace.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Observe also what remark he adds, by which he thinks that his position is confirmed: “No will,” says he, “can take away that which is proved to be inseparably implanted in nature.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Whence then comes that utterance: “So then ye cannot do the things that ye would?”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Whence also this: “For what good I would, that I do not; but what evil I hate, that do I?”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Where is that capacity which is proved to be inseparably implanted in nature?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: See, it is human beings who do not what they will; and it is about not sinning, certainly, that he was treating,—not about not flying, because it was men not birds, that formed his subject.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Behold, it is man who does not the good which he would, but does the evil which he would not: “to will is present with him, but how to perform that which is good is not present.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Where is the capacity which is proved to be inseparably implanted in nature?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For whomsoever the apostle represents by himself, if he does not speak these things of his own self, he certainly represents a man by himself.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: By our author, however, it is maintained that our human nature actually possesses an inseparable capacity of not at all sinning.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Such a statement, however, even when made by a man who knows not the effect of his words (but this ignorance is hardly attributable to the man who suggests these statements for unwary though God-fearing men), causes the grace of Christ to be “made of none effect,” since it is pretended that human nature is sufficient for its own holiness and justification.
